• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Who Gets to be the Main Character? A Data Wrangling Tutorial

Grace Schneider

3.30.26

# Load any R Packages you may need
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(ggplot2)
library(mosaic)
# Load any datasets you may need

movies <- read_csv("movies.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  rename(raw1 = 1, raw2 = 2, junk = 3) %>%
  select(-junk) %>%
  separate(
    raw1,
    into = c("name", "rating", "genre", "year", "released"),
    sep = ";",
    extra = "merge",
    fill = "right"
  ) %>%
  separate(
    raw2,
    into = c("country_info", "score", "votes", "director", "writer",
             "star", "country", "budget", "gross", "company", "runtime"),
    sep = ";",
    extra = "merge",
    fill = "right"
  ) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ str_remove_all(.x, '"'))) %>% 
  mutate(
    year = as.numeric(year),
    score = as.numeric(score),
    votes = as.numeric(votes),
    budget = as.numeric(budget),
    gross = as.numeric(gross),
    runtime = as.numeric(runtime)
  ) %>% 
  mutate(
    genre = str_trim(genre),
    rating = str_trim(rating)
  )

What is Data Wrangling?

If you have ever seen a dataset and thought ‘this is a mess’ you know understand the purpose why data wrangling exists. Data wrangling is the process of transforming raw, messy data into something clean and structured enough to analyze. Real-world data almost never arrives in a format that is ready to use. Through this ‘how to’ we will be using a dataset of 4,000 Hollywood movies released between 1980 and 2001 to answer one guiding question: ‘Which actors dominated Hollywood as lead stars, and did starring in more movies actually lead to better-rated films?’

In other words, who keeps getting to be the main character, and is it deserved? Every wrangling step we take will be motivated by what we need to know next to answer that question. By the end, you’ll see the full pipeline, from raw data to a completed visualization!

The Dataset

The data comes from Kaggle, originally sourced from IMDB. It contains one row per film with 15 variables covering the film’s title, cast, finances, and audiences rating.

How To Data Wrangle

Step 1: The Mess Before the Magic

When we load this dataset with the standard read.csv(), R misreads the file because it uses semicolons as separators instead of commas, and wraps values in extra quote characters. Rather than accepting a broken result, we can write our own custom function to handle it correctly. A function in R lets you package a set of steps and reuse them. Here we write parse_row() which uses three base R tools:

gsub() to strip out unwanted quote characters strsplit() to split each row at the semicolon trimws() to clean up any extra whitespace

We then apply it to every line of the raw file using lapply().

name rating genre year released score votes director writer star country budget gross company runtime
The Shining R Drama 1980 June 13, 1980 (United States) 8.4 927000.0 Stanley Kubrick Stephen King Jack Nicholson United Kingdom 19000000 46998772.0 Warner Bros. 146.0,
The Blue Lagoon R Adventure 1980 July 2, 1980 (United States) 5.8 65000.0 Randal Kleiser Henry De Vere Stacpoole Brooke Shields United States 4500000 58853106.0 Columbia Pictures 104.0,
Star Wars: Episode V - The Empire Strikes Back PG Action 1980 June 20, 1980 (United States) 8.7 1200000.0 Irvin Kershner Leigh Brackett Mark Hamill United States 18000000 538375067.0 Lucasfilm 124.0,
Airplane! PG Comedy 1980 July 2, 1980 (United States) 7.7 221000.0 Jim Abrahams Jim Abrahams Robert Hays United States 3500000 83453539.0 Paramount Pictures 88.0,
Caddyshack R Comedy 1980 July 25, 1980 (United States) 7.3 108000.0 Harold Ramis Brian Doyle-Murray Chevy Chase United States 6000000 39846344.0 Orion Pictures 98.0,
Friday the 13th R Horror 1980 May 9, 1980 (United States) 6.4 123000.0 Sean S. Cunningham Victor Miller Betsy Palmer United States 550000 39754601.0 Paramount Pictures 95.0,
The Blues Brothers R Action 1980 June 20, 1980 (United States) 7.9 188000.0 John Landis Dan Aykroyd John Belushi United States 27000000 115229890.0 Universal Pictures 133.0,
Raging Bull R Biography 1980 December 19, 1980 (United States) 8.2 330000.0 Martin Scorsese Jake LaMotta Robert De Niro United States 18000000 23402427.0 Chartoff-Winkler Productions 129.0,
Superman II PG Action 1980 June 19, 1981 (United States) 6.8 101000.0 Richard Lester Jerry Siegel Gene Hackman United States 54000000 108185706.0 Dovemead Films 127.0,
The Long Riders R Biography 1980 May 16, 1980 (United States) 7.0 10000.0 Walter Hill Bill Bryden David Carradine United States 10000000 15795189.0 United Artists 100.0,

This is a prime example of terribly messy data! Rather than 15 individual columns, R loaded the entire row as a single column, with two left over junk columns (x,x.1). Before we can do any proper analysis, we need to clean the data. This cleaning process is itself a key piece of data wrangling! To fix this, we’ll write a custom function called parse_row() that reads the file line by line and correctly splits each row into its 15 columns. This function uses three base R tools: gsub() to strip out unwanted quote characters, strsplit() to split each row at the semicolon separator, and trimws() to clean up any extra whitespace. We then apply it to every line of the raw file using lapply().

Step 2: Marie Kondo Your Columns

Now that our data is properly structured, we can start shaping it for our analysis. select() lets you choose which columns to keep. Our question is about lead stars and their scores, so we don’t need every variable — things like writer, company, and released won’t help us answer it. Trimming down makes the dataset easier to read and work with. The rule of thumb –> only keep columns you’ll actually use!

name star genre year score budget gross rating
The Shining Jack Nicholson Drama 1980 8.4 19000000 46998772.0 R
The Blue Lagoon Brooke Shields Adventure 1980 5.8 4500000 58853106.0 R
Star Wars: Episode V - The Empire Strikes Back Mark Hamill Action 1980 8.7 18000000 538375067.0 PG
Airplane! Robert Hays Comedy 1980 7.7 3500000 83453539.0 PG
Caddyshack Chevy Chase Comedy 1980 7.3 6000000 39846344.0 R
Friday the 13th Betsy Palmer Horror 1980 6.4 550000 39754601.0 R
The Blues Brothers John Belushi Action 1980 7.9 27000000 115229890.0 R
Raging Bull Robert De Niro Biography 1980 8.2 18000000 23402427.0 R
Superman II Gene Hackman Action 1980 6.8 54000000 108185706.0 PG
The Long Riders David Carradine Biography 1980 7.0 10000000 15795189.0 R
Any Which Way You Can Clint Eastwood Action 1980 6.1 15000000 70687344.0 PG
The Gods Must Be Crazy N!xau Adventure 1980 7.3 5000000 30031783.0 PG
Popeye Robin Williams Adventure 1980 5.3 20000000 49823037.0 PG
Ordinary People Donald Sutherland Drama 1980 7.7 6000000 54766923.0 R
Dressed to Kill Michael Caine Crime 1980 7.1 6500000 31899000.0 R

We’ve gone from 15 columns down to 8. Notice we used the pipe operator %>% to pass the result of one step directly into the next, this is the foundation of readable tidyverse code and you’ll see it in every single step from here on.

Step 3: Teaching R What a Number Actually Is

Currently every variable is being treated as a character (shown through ), meaning r is treating numbers like score and budget as plain text. Making it impossible for us to do any mathematical analysis. mutate() is the function for creating or modifying columns. Here we will use it to convert these columns to numeric variables rather than integers.

name star genre year score budget gross rating
The Shining Jack Nicholson Drama 1980 8.4 19000000 46998772 R
The Blue Lagoon Brooke Shields Adventure 1980 5.8 4500000 58853106 R
Star Wars: Episode V - The Empire Strikes Back Mark Hamill Action 1980 8.7 18000000 538375067 PG
Airplane! Robert Hays Comedy 1980 7.7 3500000 83453539 PG
Caddyshack Chevy Chase Comedy 1980 7.3 6000000 39846344 R
Friday the 13th Betsy Palmer Horror 1980 6.4 550000 39754601 R

Now score, budget, gross, show as (numeric) and year as (integer). Meaning we are now able to do mathematical equations with these columns! Without this step we would be unable to calculate avgs, sort by score, or compare movie budgets.

Step 4: Kicking Out the Zeros

Real data almost always has missing values. Before analyzing anything we need to understand what’s missing and decide what to do about it.

missing_score missing_budget missing_gross zero_budget
0 0 169 1392

Looking at the output, budget has 1,392 zero values and gross has 169 missing values. A $0 budget almost certainly means the data was not recorded — not that the film actually cost nothing to make. We’ll treat these zeros as missing and remove them along with any rows where gross is NA, since we need both values for our analysis. filter() keeps only the rows where a condition is TRUE — think of it like a sieve, rows that don’t pass fall through and are removed. The !is.na() notation means “is NOT missing.”

## [1] 2608

After filtering we’re left with 2,608 rows, down from 4,000. Every remaining row now has a valid score, real budget, and real gross value. Our dataset is clean and ready for deeper analysis.

Step 5: Making New Meaning from Old Data

mutate() isn’t just for fixing data types, it’s also how you create brand new variables from existing ones. Now that our numeric columns are properly formatted, we can calculate something new and meaningful: profit. By subtracting budget from gross we get a direct measure of how commercially successful each film was not just how much it made, but how much it made above what it cost.

name budget gross profit
Titanic 200000000 2201647264 2001647264
The Lion King 45000000 1083720877 1038720877
Jurassic Park 63000000 1033928303 970928303
Star Wars: Episode I - The Phantom Menace 115000000 1027082707 912082707
Harry Potter and the Sorcerer’s Stone 125000000 1006968171 881968171

Here we also used arrange() which is another core verb. arrange(desc(…)) sort rows from highest to lowest by a given column. The combination of select() %>% alongside arrange() %>% head() is a really handy pattern for quickly viewing the top of your data. Not surprisingly, some of the biggest blockbusters of this era are at the top (YAY JURASSIC PARK!)

Step 6: Who keeps Showing Up?

Now we get to the core of the question: who keeps getting cast as the lead? count() is a shortcut that groups your data by a variable and counts how many rows belong to each group. It’s equivalent to writing group_by() %>% summarize (n=n()) but much quicker to write.

star n
Robert De Niro 24
Sylvester Stallone 23
Arnold Schwarzenegger 21
Bruce Willis 21
Mel Gibson 21
Tom Hanks 21
Robin Williams 20
Clint Eastwood 18
Eddie Murphy 18
Harrison Ford 18
John Travolta 18
Jean-Claude Van Damme 17
Kevin Costner 17
Tom Cruise 17
Chevy Chase 16

Robert De Niro, Mel Gibson, Tom Hanks… the same names appear again and again. The lead role in Hollywood from 1980 - 2001 went to a very small, recurring pool of actors. This already starts to answer the question, but there is another question that begins to arise as the top 15 are males. Where are the women?!

Step 7: Where are the Women?!

A great thing about filter() is that we can use it with %in% to search for a specific list of values. Let’s use it to look specifically at well-known actresses from this era and count how many films each one led. The %in% operator checks whether a value appears anywhere in a list; think of it like asking “is this name on my list?”

star n
Meryl Streep 13
Julia Roberts 9
Sandra Bullock 8
Demi Moore 7
Sigourney Weaver 7
Goldie Hawn 6
Jodie Foster 6
Michelle Pfeiffer 6
Diane Keaton 5
Sally Field 5
Susan Sarandon 5
Whoopi Goldberg 5
Kathleen Turner 4
Geena Davis 3
Meg Ryan 3

Now compare these numbers to the top male stars from Step 6 the difference is wild. The most frequently cast actresses appear far less often than their male counterparts. Robert De Niro alone led more films than almost every actress on this list combined. This directly answers our guiding question, in Hollywood from 1980–2001, the main character slot was not equally distributed at all.

Step 8: More Films = Better Films? T or F…

We know who appeared most often, but did starring in more films mean better rated films? summarise() collapses many rows into a single summary row. Combined with group_by() it computes a summary for each group separately, which is one of the most useful patterns in the tidyverse. Here we group by star and compute how many films they led and their average IMDb score.

star appearences avg_score
Robert De Niro 24 6.98
Sylvester Stallone 23 5.99
Arnold Schwarzenegger 21 6.31
Bruce Willis 21 6.62
Mel Gibson 21 6.83
Tom Hanks 21 7.12
Robin Williams 20 6.45
Clint Eastwood 18 6.56
Eddie Murphy 18 5.85
Harrison Ford 18 6.94
John Travolta 18 5.82
Jean-Claude Van Damme 17 5.56
Kevin Costner 17 6.89
Tom Cruise 17 6.71
Chevy Chase 16 6.05

Robert De Niro leads with 24 appearances and an avg score of 6.98, while Sylvester Stallone has 23 appearances but a notably lower avg score of 5.99. Tom Hanks stands out with the highest avg score of 7.12 despite tying appearances with several other actors, which suggests his films were more consistently well received. This is exactly what we discussed prior, more appearences does not automatically equal better films.

Step 9: Trimming the Guest List

We have our full summary table but we want to focus on just the top 15 most featured stars for our visualization. slice_max() is a clean and explicit way to say “give me the top N rows by this variable.” We then use fct_inorder() from the forcats package to lock in the order of the star names so our chart displays them sorted by average score rather than alphabetically.

star appearences avg_score
Jean-Claude Van Damme 17 5.56
John Travolta 18 5.82
Eddie Murphy 18 5.85
Sylvester Stallone 23 5.99
Chevy Chase 16 6.05
Arnold Schwarzenegger 21 6.31
Robin Williams 20 6.45
Clint Eastwood 18 6.56
Bruce Willis 21 6.62
Tom Cruise 17 6.71
Mel Gibson 21 6.83
Kevin Costner 17 6.89
Harrison Ford 18 6.94
Robert De Niro 24 6.98
Tom Hanks 21 7.12

Looking at this table tells a clear story. Robert De Niro sits at the top with 24 appearances, but it’s Tom Hanks who has the highest average IMDb score of 7.12 meaning fewer films but better ones. On the other end, Sylvester Stallone has 23 appearances but the lowest average score in the top 15. The data suggests that the actors who got the most opportunities weren’t always the ones delivering the best work. Now that we have this clean, summarized table, we’re ready to visualize these patterns. A table tells us the numbers, a chart will tell us the story.

Step 10: Flipping the Table (on purpose)

Right now budget and gross are separate columns, they are currently in a wide format. But to compare them side by side in a single ggplot chart we need them stacked into one column, placing them in a long format through pivot_longer(). It takes multiple columns and collapses them into two, one holding the variable name and the other holding the value.

star type amount
Chevy Chase budget 6000000
Chevy Chase gross 39846344
Robert De Niro budget 18000000
Robert De Niro gross 23402427
Clint Eastwood budget 15000000
Clint Eastwood gross 70687344
Robin Williams budget 20000000
Robin Williams gross 49823037
Clint Eastwood budget 6500000
Clint Eastwood gross 24265659

Notice each film now takes up two rows instead of one, one for its budget and one for its gross. The names_to argument names the new column holding the old column names (“budget” or “gross”), and values_to names the column holding the actual dollar amounts. Without this reshaping step, ggplot2 would have no way to color budget and gross differently in the same chart. This is one of the most common reasons to use pivot_longer(); whenever you want to compare multiple numeric columns visually, long format is almost always what you need.

Step 11: Visualization: Show Me the Money

Now we can finally plot the reshaped data. This chart shows the average budget versus average gross revenue for each of our top 15 stars. This would have been impossible to make without pivot_longer() by reshaping the data into long format, ggplot2 can automatically color budget and gross differently using fill = type.

Most of our top stars earned back their budgets and then some but the margins vary widely. Tom Hanks and Harrison Ford show the largest gap between budget and gross, suggesting their films were not only frequent but highly profitable. Robert De Niro on the other hand shows a much smaller gap despite leading the most films. A big name and a big paycheck don’t always mean a big return.

Step 12: Visualization: And the Award Goes To…

This is our final and most important visualization the one that brings everything together. Every wrangling step we took was building toward this moment. The chart below shows the 15 most frequently cast lead stars from 1980–2001. Bar length shows how many films they led and the color shows their average IMDb score, answering both parts of our question at once.

Conclusion

What did We Learn?

These two charts together paint a clear picture of Hollywood from 1980–2001. The main character slot was narrow. The top 15 most featured lead stars are all male. Compare that to our actress analysis in Step 7 Meryl Streep, the most frequently cast actress in this dataset, led 13 films. Robert De Niro led 24. The gap speaks for itself.

More appearances does not equal better films. Tom Hanks sits at the top of our chart with the deepest purple bar — the highest average IMDb score of 7.12, while Sylvester Stallone has 23 appearances but one of the lowest average scores. Volume and quality are not the same thing.

Most top stars earned back their budgets, but not all. Our budget vs. gross chart showed that while most top stars’ films were profitable on average, the margins varied widely. A famous face doesn’t automatically guarantee a big return.

Why Data Wrangling Matters

Data wrangling isn’t just a technical skill it’s how you turn a messy, unusable file into a story worth telling. When we first loaded this dataset, R gave us 3 broken columns and thousands of misread values. By the time we finished, we had uncovered real patterns about power, representation, and quality in Hollywood. Every step we took had a purpose:

readLines() and parse_row() turned an unreadable file into structured data select() focused our attention on what mattered mutate() gave us the right data types and new variables to work with filter() removed noise so our analysis could be trusted count() and summarise() revealed who dominated and how well they performed pivot_longer() reshaped our data so we could visualize it properly

None of the insights from our charts would have been possible without these steps. That is the real power of data wrangling not the functions themselves, but the clarity they create. The next time you open a messy dataset, you now have the tools to turn it into something meaningful.

References

Data

Clean Data

Functions

Scientific Notation
